Roman Aqueducts

The Romans built aqueducts to carry water from distant sources into cities,
using gravity alone. Water flowed down a very gradual slope along channels of
stone and concrete. The first Roman aqueduct, the Aqua Appia, was built in 312
BCE. Where valleys had to be crossed, the Romans built arched bridges to keep
the channel at the correct gentle gradient. At its height the city of Rome was
served by eleven aqueducts supplying public baths, fountains, and private homes.
